coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots disrupt the mitochondrial potential of Trypanosoma brucei by interacting with the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1 after endocytosis via the VSG recycling pathway

coredata.dc:description:
Hypothesis Trypanosomiasis is one of the main threats to human and animal health in African countries. Trypanosoma brucei can evade the host immune recognition by rapidly altering its variant surface glycoprotein (VSG). The ATP synthase F1 subunit of the parasite exhibits extremely low similarity to that of its mammalian hosts, hypothetically making it an ideal target for the development of novel therapeutics.
Experiments Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized, and their adhesion to T. brucei surface and internalization was observed microscopically. The activity of ATP synthase and mitochondrial membrane potential of T. brucei were measured after exposure to GQDs. Proteomics, biolayer interferometry, and molecular dynamic simulations were utilized to evaluate the interaction between GQDs with the target proteins.
Findings GQDs specifically adhered to the VSG of T. brucei and were conveyed inside the parasite via the VSG internalization pathway. The GQDs promoted intracellular ROS production, interacted with, and inhibited the activity of the p18 subunit of ATP synthase, disrupted parasite mitochondrial membrane potential. Additionally, the GQDs caused a decrease in aminoacyl – tRNA biosynthesis, and upregulated RNA and protein degradation pathways. The findings of this study offer a novel avenue for the target-oriented discovery of anti-trypanosome drugs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs and evaluation of their cytotoxicity. (a) TEM image of GQDs, Scale bar = 20 nm. (b) HRTEM image of a single GQD shown in the dished pentagon. Scale bar = 3 nm. (c) The distribution of GQD diameters (n = 523) was analyzed, revealing an average diameter of 3.01 nm. (d) After coincubation of GQDs with HepG2 cells for 12 and 24 h, cell viability was assessed, with IC50 values of 512.9 and 359.1 μg/mL, respectively. The corresponding dose–response curves are shown in Fig. S1d. (e) Following coincubation of GQDs with T. brucei for 12 and 24 h, the viability of the parasites was evaluated, with IC50 values of 29.40 and 6.582 μg/mL, respectively. ***P < 0.001 versus control. The dose–response curves are presented in Fig. S1d.

Distribution of GQDs in T. brucei. (a)T. brucei were fixed using 4 % paraformaldehyde and subsequently incubated with GQDs at concentrations of 250 and 500 μg/mL to investigate the binding of GQDs to the parasites. Untreated T. brucei served as a control. The results demonstrated that GQDs bound specifically to the surface of T. brucei, with the binding exhibiting a concentration-dependent manner. (b) Fluorescence microscopy was used to visualize the uptake and distribution of GQDs (50 μg/mL) in T. brucei cells after incubation for 12 and 24 h. Untreated T. brucei cells served as a control. At 12 h, the GQDs predominantly accumulated in the flagellar pocket region, while at 24 h, they exhibited a diffuse distribution within the cells. Scale bar = 5 μm.

TEM examination demonstrates GQDs-induced mitochondrial damage in T. brucei, accompanied by late-stage apoptosis, elevated ROS levels, depolarization of mitochondrial membrane potential, and reduction in ATP synthase activity. (a) TEM images of T. brucei after incubation with 50 μg/mL GQDs for 12 and 24 h. The images reveal that as the incubation time increased, the extent of mitochondrial swelling also became prominent. The labeled organelles are as follows: N, nucleus; F, flagellum; M, mitochondria. Scale bar = 500 nm. (b) The T. brucei apoptosis profile was assessed using the Annexin V/PI assay following coincubation with GQDs for 12 and 24 h. Representative scatter plots are provided in Fig. S2. The apoptosis levels demonstrated a correlation with both the duration of exposure and the concentration of GQDs. (c) The T. brucei intracellular ROS levels were measured following incubation with GQDs for durations of 12 and 24 h. The ROS levels were correlated with both the duration of exposure and the concentration of GQDs. (d) Significant decrease in the mitochondrial membrane potential of T. brucei after 24 h of incubation with GQDs at various concentrations. (e) Changes in ATP synthase ATP hydrolytic activity in T. brucei after 12 and 24 h of incubation with different concentrations of GQDs. The results demonstrated that GQDs caused a reduction in ATP synthase hydrolytic activity in a time-dependent and concentration-dependent manner.

Colocalization of ATP synthase F1 p18 subunit protein by indirect immunofluorescence, and identification of GQD-interactome. (a) Fluorescence images were captured using an anti-ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 antibody and a mitochondrial marker antibody (anti-ATP synthase subunit alpha). The scatter plots below represent the calculated Manders' coefficients for the corresponding images, with Manders' M1 representing the fraction of the green signal that overlapped with the red signal and Manders' M2 representing the fraction of the red signal that overlapped with the green signal, the two proteins were colocalized. Scale bar = 5 μm. (b) 71 proteins that directly interacted with GQDs were identified via proteomic analysis on the proteins pulled-down with the GQD-conjugated beads. The circular diameters of these proteins represent their abundance values, which have been log2 transformed. The colors indicate the p values of the proteins. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

BLI analysis of the interaction between GQDs with VSG and the ATP synthase F1 p18 subunit proteins, along with their corresponding MD simulation results. (a) BLI was employed to investigate the binding and dissociation kinetics between GQDs and VSG, and a KD = 6.08 × 10−7 ± 1.59 × 10−8 and an R2 = 0.9644, indicating a strong binding affinity. (b) Similarly, BLI was used to characterize the binding and dissociation kinetics between GQDs and ATP synthase F1 subunit p18, yielding a KD = 2.1 × 10−7 ± 2.62 × 10−9 and an R2 = 0.9828, indicating a strong binding affinity. (c) The RMSD was calculated from the MD simulations of the docked complex of the GQDs bound to VSG. The simulation reached equilibrium after approximately 40 ns, with the red line representing the running average over every 2000 frames. (d) Similarly, the RMSD was computed from the MD simulations of the docked complex of ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 and GQDs. The simulation reached equilibrium after approximately 20 ns, with the red line representing the running average over every 2000 frames. (e) Strong interaction forces between the VSG and GQDs were analyzed using the model extracted from the last frame of the MD simulation. (f) Similarly, strong interaction forces between ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 and GQDs were analyzed using the model extracted from the last frame of the corresponding MD simulation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Heatmap of dynamic clustering for T. brucei proteins (p < 0.05, fold change > 1.5) after exposure to GQDs. A fuzzy clustering algorithm was employed to cluster the expression profiles. The line plots and circular heatmaps illustrate the changes in protein abundance at 0, 6, 12, and 24 h after exposure to GQDs. In the line plots, the chart below each cluster displays the main enriched functional terms, which represent KEGG pathway and domain enrichment. The color of the line indicates the membership of the proteins to the current cluster, with the red color indicating high membership and blue color indicating low membership. The upward and downward trends of the lines represent the relative changes in protein expression levels. In the circular heatmap, the blue color represents lower abundance, while red represents higher abundance. The 6 sections of the heatmap correspond to 6 clusters classified according to protein abundance trends. n denotes the number of proteins allocated within the current cluster. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots disrupt the mitochondrial potential of Trypanosoma brucei by interacting with the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1 after endocytosis via the VSG recycling pathway

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The figure was created using Biorender. Publishing license number: WR27EYKHVI.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trypanosomiasis is one of the main threats to human and animal health in African countries. Trypanosoma brucei can evade the host immune recognition by rapidly altering its variant surface glycoprotein (VSG). The ATP synthase F1 subunit of the parasite exhibits extremely low similarity to that of its mammalian hosts, hypothetically making it an ideal target for the development of novel therapeutics.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized, and their adhesion to T. brucei surface and internalization was observed microscopically. The activity of ATP synthase and mitochondrial membrane potential of T. brucei were measured after exposure to GQDs. Proteomics, biolayer interferometry, and molecular dynamic simulations were utilized to evaluate the interaction between GQDs with the target proteins.

GQDs specifically adhered to the VSG of T. brucei and were conveyed inside the parasite via the VSG internalization pathway. The GQDs promoted intracellular ROS production, interacted with, and inhibited the activity of the p18 subunit of ATP synthase, disrupted parasite mitochondrial membrane potential. Additionally, the GQDs caused a decrease in aminoacyl – tRNA biosynthesis, and upregulated RNA and protein degradation pathways. The findings of this study offer a novel avenue for the target-oriented discovery of anti-trypanosome drugs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data are available in the main text or the supplementary materials which can be download at the website (http://download.biorawdata.com/ftbz/). The MS spectrometry measurement files have been deposited at the ProteomeXchange consortium (http://proteomecentral.proteomexchange.org) via the iProX partner repository with the dataset. All source data are available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Trypanosoma brucei and its subspecies are flagellar protozoan parasites that cause human or animal trypanosomiasis. T. b. brucei infects only animals, causing the disease known as Nagana, while T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense are the causative agents of human African trypanosomiasis (HAT), also known as sleeping sickness [1]. The latter two diseases have severe consequences for public health in African countries [2]. Moreover, T. brucei has emerged as a versatile and powerful experimental model for studying molecular and cellular aspects of parasitology [3,4].

Currently, there are still tremendous challenges in the prevention and treatment of trypanosomiasis due to the parasite's ability of seemingly unlimited antigenic variation to cause seemingly unlimited antigenic variation and the emergence of drug resistance [5], which indicates a need for the discovery of innovative solutions.

Variant surface glycoproteins (VSGs), encoded by a large gene family, are highly variable molecules that form a dense covering over the surface of each T. brucei bloodstream form (BSF) [6]. These VSGs are anchored to the membrane via glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) and account for more than 90 % of the cell surface proteome, which efficiently shield the underlying invariant membrane proteins from host immune recognition [7]. The T. brucei BSF harnesses hydrodynamic forces to direct VSGs toward the flagellar pocket, promoting the recycling of existing VSGs and the generation of new VSGs, enabling the switching of surface antigens within minutes and allowing the parasite to evade the host immune response [8]. The rapid recycling and renewal of VSGs through the flagellar pocket is the primary immune evasion mechanism employed by T. brucei [9]. The mitochondrial ATP synthase of T. brucei exhibits different functions in different life cycle stages, it is involved in ATP synthesis in the tsetse fly and ATP hydrolysis in the mammalian host to maintain the mitochondrial membrane potential [10]. The switch between these two functions is dependent on the inhibitory factor 1 (tbIF1), which is expressed exclusively in the procyclic form (PCF) stage to inhibit ATP hydrolysis. Notably, induced expression of tbIF1 in the bloodstream form (BSF) stage results in lethality, underlying the critical importance of ATP hydrolysis by the ATP synthase for the survival of the BSF [11]. Moreover, the ATP synthase F1 subunit p18, which is absent in mammals, is indispensable for the activity of this synthase [12]. Suppression of the ATP synthase subunit p18 expression markedly impairs the viability of T. brucei in both its insect and mammalian hosts, leading to a decrease in the mitochondrial membrane potential and a reduction in the hydrolytic activity of intracellular enzymes [13]. These findings elucidate the essential role of the p18 subunit in maintaining the viability of trypanosomes during the blood phase. Given that the p18 subunit has lower homology with mammalian proteins, it has emerged as a promising drug target for the treatment of trypanosomiasis [13].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as reported by K.S. Jaiswal et al., have attracted significant attention due to their distinctive structural characteristics, nanoscale dimensions, and superior fluorescent properties, demonstrating considerable promise for diverse biomedical applications [14,15]. Numerous cytotoxicity assays have confirmed that GQDs have low toxicity to a wide range of cell types [16–18]. Furthermore, recent clinical trials involving oxidized graphene have emphasized its safety for use in humans [19].

In this study, we demonstrate that GQDs are capable of penetrating trypanosome cells via the endocytosis of VSGs of T. brucei. We show that these quantum dots subsequently target and specifically bind to the p18 subunit of T. brucei F0F1-ATP synthase, leading to mitochondrial membrane potential collapse, a decrease in aminoacyl – tRNA biosynthesis, downregulation of the proteasome pathway, and upregulation of the RNA degradation pathway. These findings elucidate a novel anti-trypanosomal mechanism of GQDs, offering new theoretical and practical insights for the advancement of nanomedicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Ethics statement
ce:section.ce:para: This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of Shenyang Agricultural University (protocol number 202203A012). All animal procedures, interventions, and husbandry were conducted in strict accordance with the guidelines established by the Laboratory Animal Center of Shenyang Agricultural University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanofibers, Dihydroethidium (DHE) fluorescent dye, JC-1 dye, radio immunoprecipitation assay (RIPA) buffer, protease and phosphatase inhibitor cocktail, and 4,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Merck (Germany). Protein A and Protein G beads, along with His GraviTrap, were acquired from Cytiva (USA). The BCA protein concentration assay kit, fetal bovine serum, penicillin–streptomycin, trypsin, high-glucose Dulbecco's modified eagle medium, PrestoBlue Cell Viability Reagent, Annexin V-FITC, propidium iodide, TMT10ple isobaric label reagent sets and the ExpiFectamine 293 Transfection Kit were supplied by Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Carboxylated magnetic beads, 4-morpholineethanesulfonic acid buffer and amino-PEG6-amine came from Aladdin (China). All remaining chemicals were sourced from Sigma–Aldrich, except for any specified earlier from different suppliers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanofibers (0.3 g) were added to 90 mL of sulfuric acid and 30 mL of nitric acid. The black suspension underwent ultrasonic dispersion for 2 h and was stirred at 110 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the mixture cooled and was diluted with 600 mL of deionized water. The pH was adjusted to 7.5 using sodium carbonate, and the solution was placed in a dialysis bag (2 kDa cutoff) for 3 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and HRTEM images were obtained with an FEI Tecnai TF30 microscope (Netherlands). Particle size was analyzed using ImageJ software, version 1.54f. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded at room temperature with a fluorescence spectrophotometer. A quartz cuvette was cleaned and dried, then 3 mL of the GQDs suspension was added. An F-280 spectrophotometer from Gangdong Technology was used to determine excitation and emission wavelengths and fluorescence intensity. Data were analyzed with ZWin software. The quantum yield was measured using the Edinburgh FLS-1000 spectro-fluorimeter. Based on the TEM measurement data and models reported in the literature [20], the molecular weight of GQDs was calculated to be 5280.524 g/M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cultivation of T. brucei and HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: BSF T. b. brucei Lister 427 strain was cultured in HMI-9 supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin. Human HepG2 cells were maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin. Both cell types were cultured at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay
ce:section.ce:para: For HepG2 cells, GQDs were diluted in DMEM to final concentrations of 2, 4, 7, 15, 30, 60, 125, 250, 500, 1000, and 2000 μg/mL and incubated for 12 and 24 h. For T. brucei, GQDs were diluted in HMI-9 medium to final concentrations of 7, 15, 30, 60, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μg/mL and incubated for 12 and 24 h. PrestoBlue cell viability reagent was added before the data was read. The plate was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. The absorbance at 570 nm and 600 nm was measured using a Tecan Infinite 200Pro microplate reader (Tecan, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of the GQD internalization process after incubation with T. brucei
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the localization of GQDs in T. brucei, the parasites were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde and incubated with GQDs at concentrations of 250 and 500 μg/mL to examine their binding to the parasite surface. In another experiment, T. brucei was also incubated with 50 μg/mL GQDs in HMI-9 medium, and green fluorescence images were captured at 12- and 24-hours post-incubation using a SIM-ultimate super-resolution microscope (CSR-Biotech, China) equipped with a 100 × oil immersion objective (NA, 1.5). Images were processed with the HIS-SIM IMAGER software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining assay for apoptosis detection
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were serially diluted in HMI-9 medium and incubated with T. brucei for 12 or 24 h. Cells were collected by centrifugation and resuspended in 500 μL of HBSS. Annexin V-FITC (5 μL) and propidium iodide (PI; 5 μL) were added, and the samples were incubated in dark at room temperature for 10 min. Annexin V-FITC and PI staining were analyzed by flow cytometry using a BD Aria III instrument with FITC and PI channels and appropriate compensation settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of intracellular ROS levels
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were diluted by HMI-9 medium to final concentrations of 2, 4, 7, 15, 30, 60, 125, 250 μg/mL and incubated with T. brucei for 12 and 24 h. The group without GQDs addition served as the control group. Dihydroethidium (DHE) dye was used to measure intracellular ROS. Cells were incubated in HMI-9 at 37 °C for 30 min at a concentration of 5 μM and kept in the dark. Subsequently, they were washed twice with PBS. The fluorescence intensity was immediately measured by flow cytometry. The data were standardized and analyzed relative to the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the mitochondrial membrane potential
ce:section.ce:para: Trypanosoma brucei was incubated with GQDs (25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL) in HMI-9 medium in a black 96-well plate for 24 h. The group without GQDs addition served as the control group. Subsequently, the T. brucei parasites were incubated with JC-1 dye at a concentration of 10 μg/mL at 37 °C for 15 min, which were washed three times with PBS. To quantify the signal, the ratio of fluorescence intensity at PE/FITC was measured by flow cytometry (BD Bioscience, USA) to calculate the mitochondrial membrane potential. This value was then compared with the corresponding value obtained from the control group for normalization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy analysis of GQDs-induced mitochondrial damage in T. brucei
ce:section.ce:para: The T. brucei parasites were fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde at 4 °C for 24 h, washed with PBS (pH 7.4), and preembedded in 1 % agarose. Postfixation processing procedures involved treatment with 1 % osmium tetroxide at room temperature in the dark for 2 h, followed by three buffer washes, dehydration, and samples embedded in resin. Ultrathin sections (60–80 nm) were generated and stained with 2 % uranyl acetate and 2.6 % lead citrate for contrast, and the sections were washed, dried, and analyzed under a transmission electron microscope (Tecnai-G2-F20, FEI, Netherlands).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the enzymatic activity of ATP synthase after GQD treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-ATP synthase F1 subunit α antibody was mixed into a coating solution containing 19.15 mM sodium carbonate and 34.8 mM sodium bicarbonate (pH 9.6) to a final concentration of 15 μg/mL and applied to a 96-well plate (200 μL per well) and incubated overnight at 4 °C. After washing three times with PBST, the 96-well plate were blocked with 3 % BSA. T. brucei parasites were first treated with GQDs (15, 30, 60, 125, 250 μg/mL) in HMI-9 medium containing for 12 and 24 h. A control group without GQDs was included. The parasites were then washed three times with cold PBS, and the enzyme activity was measured using the ATP synthase enzyme activity microplate assay kit according to the manufacturer's instructions. Briefly, protein solutions (in triplicate, 1 μg/μL each) were extracted and added to the antibody-coated 96-well plate. After a 3-hour incubation at room temperature, 40 μL of lipid mixture and 200 μL of reagent mixture were added to each well. The ATP synthase activity was monitored by measuring the absorbance change at 340 nm using a Tecan Infinite 200Pro spectrophotometer (Switzerland) for 60 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression of recombinant p18 and beta subunits of ATP synthase F1 and beta subunit proteins
ce:section.ce:para: The genes coding for ATP synthase F1 subunits p18 (Tb927.5.1710) and beta (Tb927.3.1380) were codon-optimized and synthesized by Sangon Biotech (China). The synthesized genes were then inserted into the pET28a expression vector. The recombinant plasmids were subsequently expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3) strains, following the methods outlined in previous research [21]. The His-tagged subunits p18 (His-p18) and beta (His-beta) were purified using Ni-NTA agarose column (Cytiva, USA) by following the standard manufacturer's protocol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression and purification of recombinant T. brucei VSG in human Expi293F cells
ce:section.ce:para: The gene sequence coding for the VSG (Tb927.4.5410) of T. brucei was cloned into the pOPING vector with an N-terminal μ-phosphatase secretion signal and a C-terminal 6 × His tag for purification. Recombinant VSG was expressed in Expi293F cells using the ExpiFectamine 293 Transfection Kit. After transfection, cells were incubated at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 on a 125 rpm shaker for three days, then at 30 °C for another three days. On day six, the supernatant with secreted VSG was collected. Purification was conducted using Co-NTA affinity chromatography on a His GraviTrap. Briefly, the column was rinsed with a buffer containing 25 mM phosphate (pH 7.5), 300 mM sodium chloride, and 5 mM imidazole, and the VSG was eluted using a 5 to 500 mM imidazole gradient, which was dialyzed against PBS for 12 h. The final protein concentration was determined by BCA protein concentration assay kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of specific antibodies to ATP synthase F1 subunit p18
ce:section.ce:para: Polyclonal antibodies targeting the ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 were generated in five BALB/c mice (female) immunized with a His-tagged version of the protein, and antibodies against the F1 alpha subunit were produced in two New Zealand white rabbits (female) using a peptide (CPIGRGQRELIVGDRQTGKTSIA) conjugated to keyhole limpet hemocyanin. Both mice and rabbits received subcutaneous immunizations four times at two-week intervals, using complete Freund's adjuvant for the first and incomplete Freund's for the subsequent immunizations to boost the immune response. Antibodies were purified from the serum using protein A and G beads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Western blot verification of antibody specificity
ce:section.ce:para: T. brucei were washed with PBS at 4 °C and lysed by ultrasonication. After lysis, 5 × SDS loading buffer was added, and the mixture was boiled at 95 °C for 10 min, then centrifuged at 12,000 RPM. The protein concentration in the supernatant was determined using a BCA assay kit. Proteins were separated via SDS-PAGE and transferred to PVDF membranes. These membranes were blocked overnight with PBST containing 3 % BSA and 0.2 % Tween 20, then incubated with primary antibodies from immunized mice and rabbits at 37 °C for an hour. HRP-conjugated goat anti-mouse/rabbit antibodies (Proteintech, China) were used as secondary antibodies. Immunoreactive bands were detected using an enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) system (Clinx, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Localization of ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 in T. brucei by indirect immunofluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the subcellular localization of the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1, T. brucei parasites were fixed, permeabilized, and blocked, followed by incubation with primary antibodies: rabbit anti-ATP synthase F1 α subunit antibody and mouse anti-ATP synthase F1 p18 subunit serum. The samples were washed three times and subsequently incubated with secondary antibodies: Alexa Fluor 594-conjugated goat anti-mouse and Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated goat anti-rabbit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). After three additional washes, the specimens were stained with DAPI. Images were acquired using a SIM-ultimate super-resolution microscope (CSR-Biotech, China) equipped with a 100 × oil immersion objective (NA, 1.5). Image reconstruction was performed using HIS-SIM IMAGER software, and colocalization analysis was conducted using ImageJ (v1.45f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of the interactive proteins of T. brucei with GQDs via a pulldown assay and label-free proteomic analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To generate GQD-conjugated magnetic beads, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide and N-hydroxysuccinimide solution were mixed in an equimolar ratio, which was then added to GQDs (2 mg/mL, 2 mL) and carboxylated magnetic beads (1.5 mL, 2.5 % w/v). After a 2-hour reaction, amino-PEG6-amine (0.5 mL, 10 mg/mL) was added, and the mixture was sonicated at 20 W for 2 h, followed by three washes with PBS. The control group underwent the same procedure but without the addition of GQDs.

In vitro cultured T. brucei were centrifuged, collected, and lysed using ultrasonication in an ice bath. The cell lysates were then incubated with GQD-conjugated magnetic beads and control beads at 37 °C for 1 h. After three PBS washes, 100 μL of PBS was added to each tube, which was then boiled at 95 °C for 10 min. The obtained protein solution was subjected to label-free proteomic analysis for protein identification, as described previously [22]. Briefly, the protein solution was digested with trypsin to obtain a polypeptide solution. Subsequently, a 2 μL aliquot of the polypeptide solution was analyzed using a nano ultra-performance liquid chromatography system (EASY-nLC1200, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) coupled with a Q-Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the interaction between T. brucei proteins and GQDs by biolayer interferometry
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the interaction kinetics of GQDs to His-tagged VSG, as well as to His-tagged ATP synthase p18 subunit, Biolayer interferometry experiments were performed using an Octet K2 system (ForteBio, USA) with the His-tagged ATP synthase beta subunit serving as an experimental control. Ni-NTA biosensors from ForteBio were pre-equilibrated in running buffer (PBS, 0.02 % Tween 20, 0.1 % BSA) and loaded with each of the two His-tagged proteins at a concentration of 300 μg/ml for 300 s. After loading, the biosensors were washed in a running buffer to remove unbound proteins. The biosensors were then exposed for 300 s with GQDs at concentrations of 125, 250, 500, 1000, and 2000 nM for VSG-His, and 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, and 4000 nM for ATP synthase p18 subunit-His and beta subunit-His, to assess the binding affinity. Subsequently, a dissociation phase was performed for 400 s in the running buffer only. Kinetic parameters were calculated by ForteBio data analysis software version 9.0 (ForteBio, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular docking
ce:section.ce:para: For the in silico protein–ligand docking simulation, the structural model for the p18 subunit was sourced from the Protein Data Bank (PDB ID: 6f5d). The configurations for both the monomer and dimer of VSG were predicted using the AlphaFold v2.3, with protein sequences obtained from the UniProt database (Q580P5). The GQD model for the GQDs was developed in accordance with methodologies outlined in a previous study [20]. Initial preparation of the models involved the removal of water molecules and the addition of hydrogen atoms using PyMOL software (version 2.5). Docking simulations were conducted using GNINA software, applying a blind docking strategy. The exhaustive search parameter was adjusted to 64 to enhance the thoroughness of the docking search. We selected the best-scoring results based on docking efficacy for further analysis, ensuring a comprehensive evaluation of potential binding affinities and configurations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamics simulation
ce:section.ce:para: The best-scoring models from docking simulations were used as initial structures for 100 ns all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using GROMACS-2019 and the Amber14SB force field [23]. Each ligand–protein complex was solvated in a cubic box with TIP3P water, ensuring a 10 Å distance from the protein to the box sides [24]. Ligand force field parameters were generated using the Antechamber Python Parser [25]. Simulations applied periodic boundary conditions, standard ionization states at pH 7.0, and were neutralized with K+ and Cl− ions using a Monte-Carlo method. [26].

MD simulations proceeded in three stages: first, an NVT ensemble with Berendsen temperature coupling [27]; second, an NPT ensemble at 1 atm and 310.15 K using the Parrinello-Rahman barostat [28]; finally, a 100 ns run under NPT, calculating long-range electrostatics with the particle mesh Ewald method [29]. The LINCS method constrained all covalent bonds, allowing a 2 fs integration time step [30]. Nonbounded interactions were managed using the Verlet cutoff scheme, truncating at 10 Å [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TMT-based quantitative proteomic analysis of GQD-treated parasites
ce:section.ce:para: After exposure to GQDs (50 μg/mL), T. brucei samples were collected at 6, 12, and 24 h, with no less than 107. T. brucei was not exposed to GQDs and was used as a control group. The collected samples were lysed by sonication (250 W, 10 s, 5 times) and analyzed using TMT-based quantitative proteomics, as previously described [32]. Briefly, the lysed samples were digested with trypsin to prepare peptide solutions, which were then labeled with TMT reagents. The labeled peptides were analyzed on a Thermo Orbitrap Fusion Lumos mass spectrometer coupled with an EasyLC 1200 HPLC system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). This approach allowed for the identification and quantification of changes in the T. brucei proteome following GQDs treatment in comparison to the control group. p values obtained from t-tests were corrected using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Database search
ce:section.ce:para: The generated MS/MS data were processed using the MaxQuant search engine (version 1.5.2.8). The tandem mass spectra were searched against the UniProt or VEuPath database. Trypsin/P was designated as the cleavage enzyme, with allowances for up to four missed cleavages. The mass tolerance for the precursor ions was set at 20 ppm for the initial search and decreased to 5 ppm for the main search, while the mass tolerance for fragment ions was set at 0.02 Da. The false discovery rate was set to less than 1 %, with a minimum score threshold for modified peptides established at over 40.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic clustering analysis of the differentially expressed proteins of T. brucei with GQDs for various exposure times
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the dynamic changes in protein expression patterns of T. brucei following exposure to GQDs (50 μg/mL) at different time points (6, 12, and 24 h). We used a clustering method based on the Mfuzz package, version 2.36.0. This method allows the multiassignment of quantified proteins or modification sites in different clusters. The number of clusters was set to six, and the fuzzification parameter m was set to 2. To identify the main functions of proteins in each cluster, we performed domain enrichment and pathway enrichment followed by adjustment of P values using R's p.adjust function.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The data are reported as the mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments. The means were compared using ANOVA and Student's t test, and a p value less than 0.05 was considered to indicate statistical significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
The GQDs were noncytotoxic to mammalian cells but parasiticidal to T. gondii
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized utilizing a previously described method [33]. Transmission electron microscopy revealed that the GQDs were monodisperse (Fig. 1 a). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy further revealed the morphology of the individual GQDs (Fig. 1b), showing that most of the nanoparticles had an average diameter of 3.01 nm (Fig. 1c).

The cytotoxic effects of GQDs were evaluated on HepG2 cells and T. brucei using the PrestoBlue Viability Reagent (https://www.thermofisher.com/order/catalog/product/A13262). Statistical analysis revealed that GQDs induced highly significant reductions in HepG2 cell viability at concentrations ≥60 μg/mL, while in T. brucei, highly significant decreases of viability were observed at concentrations as low as ≥2 μg/mL. This demonstrates that T. brucei is markedly more sensitive to GQDs compared to HepG2 cells (Fig. 1d and 1e). The T. brucei viability decreased in a concentration and time-dependent manner upon GQD exposure (Fig. 1e). The half-maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) for T. brucei were calculated to be 29.4 μg/mL at 12 h and 6.58 μg/mL at 24 h. Further evaluation of the effect of GQDs on HepG2 cells showed that, at concentrations that exert specific antiparasitic activity against T. brucei, GQDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity on mammalian cells (Fig. S1), suggesting a specific parasiticidal effect of GQDs on T. brucei. Additionally, the GQDs exhibited notable fluorescence properties, characterized by a maximum excitation peak at 390 nm and a maximum emission peak at 438 nm, with a quantum yield of 20.65 %, indicating their potential for bioimaging applications (Fig. S1a and b). Moreover, compared to the previously reported nanomaterial AuNC@GSH (Gold nanoclusters modified with glutathione) used to treat T. brucei, GQDs exhibit an IC50 value four times lower within 24 h. Furthermore, GQDs require only one-tenth of the concentration of AuNC@GSH to induce a 50 % apoptosis rate in T. brucei [34]. These findings demonstrate that GQDs display higher efficiency in killing parasites than AuNC@GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs were endocytosed through the flagellar pocket of T. brucei
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm that GQDs could adhere to the surface of T. brucei, the parasites were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde and subsequently incubated with GQDs at concentrations of 250 and 500 μg/mL, respectively. The intensity of fluorescence signal on T. brucei surface was in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 2 a). In addition, to observe the internalization of GQDs into T. brucei, the parasites were cultured with 50 μg/mL of GQDs. Compared to the control group of T. brucei without exposure to GQDs, those incubated with GQDs for 12 h exhibited significant fluorescence. Initially, the fluorescence was observed predominantly in the flagellar pocket region, but it later spread throughout the cytoplasm (Fig. 2b). This pattern indicates that GQDs entered T. brucei via the flagellar pocket, probably via the parasite’s VSG recycling pathway, as the parasite is covered solely with VSG on the surface. Further, it has been reported that BSF of T. brucei utilizes hydrodynamic drag force to detach VSGs. These detached VSGs are then taken up through the flagellar pocket of the parasite into the cell, where it will be completely degraded [8]. Consequently, GQDs bound to VSGs are continuously internalized into trypanosomes. With the degradation of VSG, GQD will be dissociated and released within trypanosome cells, which is likely the reason that the fluorescence signal was initially observed at the flagellar pocket region, but diffused in the cytoplasm of the parasite afterwards.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs induced depolarization of mitochondrial membrane potential and reduced ATP synthase activity in T. brucei
ce:section.ce:para: Following 12 and 24 h incubation with 50 μg/mL GQDs, alterations in the subcellular structures of T. brucei were examined with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images revealed dose-dependent mitochondrial damages characterized by swelling and rupture. Mitochondrial swelling was initially observed at the 12-hour and progressed to widespread rupture after 24 h of exposure to GQDs. Compared to untreated controls, T. brucei with GQD exposure exhibited a time-dependent increase in mitochondrial disruption, indicating that the GQDs inflicted damage on T. brucei mitochondria (Fig. 3 a).

An Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis assay kit was used to evaluate the rates of apoptosis and necrosis in T. brucei following treatment with GQDs. Data derived from flow cytometry analyses (Fig. S2) and statistical evaluations (Fig. 3b) indicated a predominant induction of late-stage apoptosis in T. brucei by GQDs. Specifically, after 24 h of exposure to 60 μg/mL GQDs, more than 50 % of the treated parasites were in late-stage apoptosis.

Furthermore, previous studies indicated that nanoparticles could generate ROS by donating or accepting electrons from intracellular and extracellular molecules, such as water and oxygen [35]. In this study, treatment of trypanosomes with GQDs also led to increased ROS levels in T. brucei (Fig. 3c). ROS can interact with both proteins and lipids leading to oxidation of the target molecules and cause damages in the mitochondrial membranes, disruption of mitochondrial integrity [36]. This leads to mitochondrial dysfunction, which is consistent with the mitochondrial membrane damage and mitochondrial membrane potential depolarization observed in our study (Fig. 3). Changes in the T. brucei mitochondrial membrane potential following incubation with GQDs at various concentrations were assessed using JC-1 dye. The ratio of fluorescence emission peaks at PE to FITC was utilized to quantify these observations. A decrease in the mitochondrial membrane potential was observed with increasing concentrations of GQDs, suggesting that the GQDs induced depolarization in the T. brucei mitochondria (Fig. 3d).

To assess the effect of GQDs on ATP synthase in T. brucei, the ATP hydrolysis activity was measured using a 96-well plate with a rabbit anti-ATP synthase antibody and an ATP synthase enzyme activity microplate assay kit. The results indicated that a decrease in ATP synthase activity correlated with increasing concentrations of GQDs and duration of exposure (Fig. 3e). These findings suggest that GQDs significantly inhibited the enzymatic activity of T. brucei ATP synthase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 is located in the mitochondria of T. brucei
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the subcellular location of the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1, antibodies against the p18 subunit and the mitochondrial marker, alpha subunit of ATP synthase, were generated. Their specificity was verified through Western blot analysis of the total protein of T. brucei (Fig. S1). Immunofluorescence detection revealed a high degree of colocalization between the p18 subunit protein and the alpha subunit of ATP synthase in the mitochondria of T. brucei. This colocalization was quantified using the Manders coefficient (Fig. 4 a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of T. brucei proteins interacting with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs targeted proteins in T. brucei were investigated by pulldown assays with the total protein extract from T. brucei followed by proteomic identification. After excluding proteins detected in the control beads without GQD conjugation, we identified a total of 71 unique proteins (Fig. 4b and Table S1). The five most abundant proteins identified were the variant surface glycoproteins (Tb927.4.5410/Tb927.4.5420), the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1 (Tb927.5.1710), trypanothione synthetase (Tb927.2.4370), and trypanothione synthetase (Tb11.v5.0653).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The GQDs avidly bound to VSG and ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 of T. brucei
ce:section.ce:para: The affinity between GQDs with a VSG and the ATP synthase F1 subunit p18 protein of T. brucei was evaluated using biolayer interferometry (BLI). BLI confirmed the binding of His-tagged VSG and p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1 to GQDs. The dissociation constants (KD) were 6.08 × 10−7 M ± 1.59 × 10−8 M for VSG and 2.1 × 10−7 M ± 2.62 × 10−9 M for the p18 subunit, reflected in correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9644 and 0.9828, respectively. (Fig. 5 a and b). No interaction was observed between GQDs and the ATP synthase F1 beta subunit protein (Fig. S5). These findings suggest that GQDs specifically bind to VSG and the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1. Molecular docking was further explored to verify these interactions, utilizing the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1 model from the Protein Data Bank (PDB ID: 6f5d) [37] and the VSG model generated by AlphaFold v2.3 [38]. Building on existing GQD models [20], the structures of GQD, VSG, and p18 were prepared using PyMOL v2.5 software before docking. Docking simulations were then carried out using Gnina v1.0.1 [39]. The results revealed that the best binding energies of GQD-VSG and GQD-p18 were −13.57 kcal/mol and −10.39 kcal/mol (Fig. S6), respectively, suggesting strong binding potentials between GQDs and these two proteins.

The best docking pose was selected as the starting point for subsequent molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Subsequent 100 ns MD simulations at 37 °C in a physiological saline environment indicated the high stability of the docking complexes, which was confirmed by analyzing root mean square deviation (RMSD) over time, showing both complexes reached equilibrium within 100 ns (Fig. 5c and d).

In the analysis of GQD and VSG interactions, the ARG-66, GLN-67, ASP-303, GLN-305, THR-308, GLU-345, LYS-349, ASP-355, and GLN-357 residues in the VSG (Tb927.4.5410) were identified in the formation of hydrogen bonds, whereas LYS-194 was involved in forming a salt bridge. The additional residues, including ASP-303, THR-313, ALA-315, ASP-355, LYS-356, and GLN-357, contributed to hydrophobic interactions (Fig. 5e). The RMSD of the GQDs and VSG stabilized after 20 ns, with the minimum intermolecular distance maintaining a consistent average of 0.177 nm. The hydrogen bond count was constant over time, with an average of 7.986 bonds formed during each observed time-frame (Fig. S7). Similarly, in the analysis of interactions between GQD and the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1, ASN-19, GLU-25, GLN-60, GLN-64, and GLU-100 were involved in hydrogen bond formation. LYS-107 and LYS-168 established salt bridges, while ALA-21, GLU-25, GLU-100, LEU-164, and ASP-166 were critical residues for hydrophobic interactions (Fig. 5f). The RMSD values reached a steady state after 10 ns, and the minimum intermolecular distance was consistently 0.168 nm. The formation of hydrogen bonds was stable over the time course, with an average of 8.495 bonds per observed time-frame (Fig. S7). The data further explained the mechanism of specific parasiticidal effect of GQDs on T. brucei.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD exposure resulted in a decrease in aminoacyl – tRNA biosynthesis, downregulation of the proteasome and upregulation of the RNA degradation pathways in T. brucei
ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative proteomics analysis on T. brucei proteins after exposure to GQDs for 6, 12, and 24 h, identified 351, 370, and 1953 differentially expressed proteins, respectively. The volcano plots depicting the overall differential protein expression are shown in Fig. S4 and revealed an increase in the number of differentially expressed proteins among the parasites with prolonged incubation time. These findings suggest that GQD-induced alterations in protein expression occur in a time-dependent manner.

The Mfuzz method [40] was applied for clustering analysis on proteins expressed differentially at various time points (6, 12, and 24 h) following exposure to GQDs. Compared to the untreated parasites, six distinct protein clusters were generated from the GQDs exposed T. brucei, representing the dynamic variations of the differentially expressed proteins within crucial biological pathways over time. Subsequently, we performed Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway and protein domain enrichment analyses on the proteins within each cluster. As depicted in Fig. 6 , an increase in the duration of GQD exposure led to a progressive alteration in proteins related to protein synthesis and degradation in T. brucei. These findings underscore the impact of GQDs on T. brucei protein regulatory mechanisms, illustrating a time-dependent modulation of protein expression related to essential biological functions.

The observed downregulation of the ribosome and aminoacyl – tRNA biosynthesis pathways, coupled with the upregulation of the RNA degradation pathway, indicates that exposure to GQDs led to a decrease in T. brucei's capabilities for transcription and protein synthesis. Notably, a significant decrease in ribosome activity was detected as early as 6 h postexposure to GQDs, whereas changes in the aminoacyl – tRNA biosynthesis and RNA degradation pathways became apparent only after 12 h, as depicted in clusters 1, 2, and 5 of Fig. 6. Previous studies have shown that there are eight classes of aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (aaRSs) in T. brucei [41], and the aaRSs are essential for the growth of T. brucei, suggesting their potential as potential targets for drug discovery. The downregulation of aaRSs by GQDs may lead to impaired protein synthesis and, consequently, reduced cell viability.

Upon T. brucei exposure to GQDs, minimal variations in proteasome activity were observed within the first 12 h. However, from 12 to 24 h postexposure, significant downregulation of most proteasome subunits was observed, as shown in cluster 5 of Fig. 6, indicating that the impact of GQDs on the protein degradation processes of T. brucei became pronounced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: This study, for the first time, demonstrated that GQDs could enter T. brucei through the VSG recycling pathway and activated intracellular ROS production. This leads to mitochondrial damage, membrane potential depolarization. GQDs can avidly bind to the p18 subunit of ATP synthase F1, which jeopardizes parasite energy production, results in the downregulation of the proteasome, ribosomal, and aminoacyl-tRNA biosynthesis pathways, and upregulation of the RNA degradation pathways, ultimately killing of the parasite. These findings provide a theoretical framework for the application of nanomaterials in the treatment of trypanosomiasis.
